original_hypothesis.title: Negative Rebasing Tokens Cause Vault Insolvency and Loss of Funds
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If a canonical token is a negative rebasing token, its balance decreases over time. The ERC20Vault tracks user deposits based on the initial balance change but does not account for subsequent rebases. Over time, the vault's actual balance will fall below the total supply of bridged tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: When users attempt to bridge back to the canonical chain, the vault will eventually run out of tokens. The first users to bridge back will receive their full initial amounts, while late withdrawers will suffer 100% loss as the vault becomes insolvent. This creates a bank run scenario.
deployment_assumptions: A negative rebasing token is bridged through the ERC20Vault
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No explicit checks in ERC20Vault to prevent bridging of rebasing tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceChange_ = t.balanceOf(address(this)) - _balance captures initial deposit but ignores future rebases

preconditions: Canonical token is a negative rebasing token
Sufficient time passes for rebase to reduce vault balance below bridged supply

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Vault balance < Bridged token total supply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First withdrawers extract more value than their pro-rata share of the rebased pool
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attackers who bridge back early steal value from users who bridge back late

economic_questions: Does the protocol intend to support rebasing tokens?
Can an attacker force a rebase to accelerate insolvency?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock negative rebasing token
Bridge tokens, trigger rebase, and attempt to withdraw all tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Avoidance of rebase losses at the expense of others

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires active bridging of the rebasing token
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a large negative rebase
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bank run on the vault, loss of funds for late users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Late withdrawers of the rebasing token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss of bridged assets
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total rebase deficit across the vault

validation_result.impact_summary: If a negative rebasing token is bridged through ERC20Vault, the vault's actual token balance decreases over time while bridged token supply stays constant, leading to insolvency where late withdrawers lose funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ERC20Vault records deposit amounts via a balance-delta measurement at deposit time in _handleMessage, then uses these recorded amounts for withdrawals in _transferTokens. For negative rebasing tokens, the vault's balance decreases after deposit due to rebases, but the bridged token supply (minted on destination chain) remains constant. There is no mechanism to prevent rebasing tokens from being bridged, no accounting adjustment for post-deposit balance changes, and no pro-rata withdrawal logic. The vault uses a simple safeTransfer of the full original amount on withdrawal, which will revert once the vault balance drops below the requested amount.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 227-230 of ERC20Vault.sol capture the initial deposit via balance delta. Lines 195-200 (_transferTokens) attempt to transfer the full recorded amount on withdrawal. No validation exists anywhere in the code to reject rebasing tokens or adjust for balance changes post-deposit.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The balance delta is captured at deposit time and encoded into the bridge message. For negative rebasing tokens, the vault's actual balance will decrease over time, but the amount recorded in the bridge message (and subsequently minted as bridged tokens on the destination) remains constant.

On the canonical chain, the vault attempts safeTransfer of the full original deposited amount. If the token has negatively rebased, the vault may not hold enough tokens, causing the transfer to revert for later withdrawers.

The sendToken function only validates amount, token address, and blacklist status. There is no check for token type (rebasing, fee-on-transfer, etc.) to prevent incompatible tokens from being bridged.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls sendToken with a negative rebasing token
2. _handleMessage captures balanceChange_ via before/after balanceOf delta (lines 227-230)
3. balanceChange_ is encoded into bridge message data (line 242)
4. Bridge.sendMessage sends the message to destination chain
5. On destination: onMessageInvocation decodes amount from message (line 161), mints bridged tokens via _transferTokens
6. Time passes, negative rebase reduces vault's token balance on source chain
7. Users bridge back: onMessageInvocation on source chain calls _transferTokens with original amount
9. Eventually safeTransfer reverts because vault balance < requested amount
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: deposit captures exact amount → bridge message encodes that amount → bridged tokens minted for that amount → rebase reduces vault balance → withdrawal attempts to send original amount → insufficient balance causes revert or first-come-first-served drainage. The invariant INV-ECON-002 (vault balance >= bridged supply) is directly violated by negative rebases.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A negative rebasing token is bridged through the ERC20Vault
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (DeployOnL1.s.sol) deploy ERC20Vault with no token whitelisting or type-checking mechanism. No deployment script configures any token restriction. The vault accepts any ERC20 token that implements the standard interface. The auxiliary deploy scripts create test tokens (FreeMintERC20, MayFailFreeMintERC20) but these are not rebasing tokens. Nothing in the deployment prevents a rebasing token from being bridged.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) A negative rebasing token (e.g., a token that periodically reduces all holder balances) exists on the canonical chain. (2) Users bridge this token through ERC20Vault. The vault captures the exact deposit amount. (3) On the destination chain, bridged tokens are minted for the full deposited amount. (4) Over time, the vault's balance of the rebasing token decreases due to negative rebases. (5) When users try to bridge back, the vault attempts safeTransfer of the original amount. (6) The first users to bridge back succeed, but as the vault balance decreases, later users face reverts or receive nothing. This is a classic bank-run/insolvency scenario caused by the vault's naive accounting model.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. If a negative rebasing token is bridged, the vault becomes progressively insolvent. Late withdrawers lose up to 100% of their bridged assets. The impact scales with the total value bridged and the magnitude/frequency of negative rebases. While the precondition requires a rebasing token to be bridged (which may not happen in practice with proper operational controls), the code provides no safeguard against it.
recommended_mitigations: Add a token whitelist or registry to ERC20Vault that explicitly approves tokens for bridging, rejecting unknown or known-incompatible token types
Implement a check in sendToken that detects rebasing behavior (e.g., by comparing balanceOf before and after a zero-value transfer, or maintaining a registry of known rebasing tokens)
Document clearly that rebasing tokens are not supported and add prominent warnings in the UI/SDK
Consider implementing share-based accounting (similar to ERC4626) where the vault tracks shares rather than absolute amounts, allowing pro-rata withdrawals
Add an admin function to pause bridging of specific tokens if a rebasing token is accidentally bridged
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock negative rebasing ERC20 token that reduces all balances by 10% on each rebase call. 2. Deploy ERC20Vault and Bridge infrastructure on two test chains. 3. User A deposits 1000 tokens into the vault via sendToken. 4. Verify 1000 bridged tokens minted on destination. 5. Trigger a rebase on the source chain, reducing vault balance to 900. 6. User A bridges back 1000 tokens. 7. Verify that the vault attempts safeTransfer(userA, 1000) but only has 900, causing revert. 8. Test with multiple users: User A deposits 500, User B deposits 500. After rebase (vault has 900), User A bridges back 500 (succeeds, vault has 400), User B tries to bridge back 500 (fails, vault only has 400).
uncertainties: Whether any negative rebasing tokens would realistically be bridged in production (operational controls may prevent this)
Whether the protocol team intentionally chose not to support rebasing tokens (design decision vs. code defect)
The exact behavior of the vault under positive rebasing tokens (excess balance would accumulate in vault with no withdrawal mechanism)
additional_testing_needed: Test with various rebasing token implementations (negative, positive, elastic supply)
Test interaction with fee-on-transfer tokens on the destination side (_transferTokens doesn't re-check balance delta)
Verify behavior when vault balance exactly equals the withdrawal amount (edge case)
Test recall/recovery paths when vault is already insolvent from rebasing
